topic: Someone learns their favorite author plagiarized their stories
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias sat motionless in his worn armchair, the pages of the newly published collection resting accusingly on the mahogany side table. His hands, usually so nimble when sketching out character arcs, were clenched so tightly his knuckles were white. He traced a line of text with a fingertip, the ink mocking him with its familiarity. A slow, hot flush crept up his neck, staining his cheeks a deep crimson. He reread the passage, the familiar rhythm of the prose now sounding like a deliberate theft. A low, ragged sound escaped his throat—a half-choked sigh that held all the frustration he couldn't articulate. He pushed himself abruptly to his feet, the chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor, and began pacing the length of his study, his movements jerky and agitated.

[story 2]

"This is impossible," Clara whispered, the sound barely audible above the distant hum of city traffic. She stared at the manuscript spread across her desk, the familiar phrasing of her beloved narratives now twisted into something hollow and derivative. Her breath hitched in her chest, a shallow, rapid intake of air. She picked up a pen, but instead of writing, she simply let it drop onto the blotter with a sharp clatter. Her gaze drifted to the window, where the afternoon light seemed too bright, too harsh. A tremor ran through her hands, making it difficult to steady the paper. She leaned back in her chair, burying her face in her hands, the pressure a desperate attempt to contain the sudden, overwhelming wave of disillusionment washing over her.

[story 3]

Marcus stood by the bookshelf, his shadow stretching long and distorted in the dim light of the library. He held the book—the one that contained the stolen words—as if it were a live wire. Every nerve in his body felt taut, vibrating with a restless energy. He turned the book over in his hands, his jaw working silently as he tried to reconcile the brilliance he once admired with the blatant dishonesty before him. A deep furrow settled between his brows, pulling his features into an expression of profound dismay. He let out a slow, deliberate breath, the air catching in his lungs, and then slowly, deliberately, he placed the book back onto the shelf, the movement conveying a heavy sense of finality and profound disappointment.
